vulnerability.title: Set Account Expiration Following Inactivity
vulnerability.description: To specify the number of days after a password expires (which signifies inactivity) until an account is permanently disabled, add or correct the following line in /etc/default/useradd: INACTIVE= If a password is currently on the verge of expiration, then day(s) remain(s) until the account is automatically disabled. However, if the password will not expire for another 60 days, then 60 days plus day(s) could elapse until the account would be automatically disabled. See the useradd man page for more information.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q shadow-utils; }; then var_account_disable_post_pw_expiration='' # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^INACTIVE") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s=%s" "$stripped_key" "$var_account_disable_post_pw_expiration" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -q -m 1 -i -e "^INACTIVE\\>" "/etc/default/useradd"; then escaped_formatted_output=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/

triage_decision.reason: This finding modifies a single configuration parameter in /etc/default/useradd to set account expiration after password inactivity. The change follows standard STIG hardening patterns and does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or filesystems. The remediation script is straightforward and targets a well-defined configuration file.

execution_details.stdout:
# useradd defaults file
GROUP=100
HOME=/home
INACTIVE=-1
EXPIRE=
SHELL=/bin/bash
SKEL=/etc/skel
CREATE_MAIL_SPOOL=yes

# useradd defaults file
GROUP=100
HOME=/home
INACTIVE=30
EXPIRE=
SHELL=/bin/bash
SKEL=/etc/skel
CREATE_MAIL_SPOOL=yes

# useradd defaults file
GROUP=100
HOME=/home
INACTIVE=30
EXPIRE=
SHELL=/bin/bash
SKEL=/etc/skel
CREATE_MAIL_SPOOL=yes

llm_verdict.message: The remediation was successful! The INACTIVE parameter has been changed from -1 to 30 in /etc/default/useradd. This ensures that accounts will now expire 30 days after password expiration, resolving the OpenSCAP finding about setting account expiration following inactivity. The change follows standard STIG hardening practices and does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or filesystems.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The proposed plan would functionally resolve the vulnerability by changing INACTIVE from -1 to 30 days. However, the remediation script provided in the vulnerability description is incomplete (cuts off mid-command) and doesn't match the simple sed -i approach described in the plan. The plan should ideally use a more robust approach like the script suggests with proper variable handling and configuration file modification patterns.
concerns: The remediation script in the vulnerability description is incomplete and cuts off mid-command
The proposed plan uses a simple sed -i approach which is less robust than the script pattern provided
No verification step is included to confirm the change was applied successfully
suggested_improvements: Use the complete remediation script pattern from the vulnerability description rather than a simple sed -i command
Add a verification step to confirm the INACTIVE parameter was changed to 30
Consider parameterizing the number of days (30) to make the script more flexible
Add error handling to ensure the configuration file exists before attempting modification
